title: Inductive Bias
concept: Inductive bias is the set of prior assumptions a model makes about the data; CNNs assume spatial locality and translation invariance, whereas ViTs make very few assumptions and must learn them from scratch.

exercises.prompt: Recap: If you slice a 224x224 image into 16x16 patches, how many tokens are in the patch sequence?
exercises.explain: Correct! 224/16 = 14 patches per side, and 14x14 = 196 total patches.

cards.title: Born with instincts
cards.body: Imagine a baby turtle that immediately crawls toward the ocean upon hatching — it has instincts hardcoded into its DNA. In AI, Convolutional Neural Networks are born with 'visual instincts' about how images work. Vision Transformers, however, are born with zero assumptions, starting completely from scratch.

cards.title: Inductive Bias
cards.body: Inductive bias refers to the assumptions a learning algorithm uses to predict outputs for unseen inputs. CNNs have strong inductive biases: locality (adjacent pixels are related) and translation invariance (a cat is a cat no matter where it is in the frame). ViTs have almost no visual inductive bias.

cards.title: Lined paper vs. blank canvas
cards.body: A CNN is like writing on lined paper: the lines (convolution rules) force you to write straight and keep things in order. A ViT is like a blank canvas: you can draw anything, anywhere. This freedom is powerful, but it means you must learn how to structure your work without any guide rails.

cards.title: Learning spatial order
cards.body: If you train a CNN on 10 cat photos, it can find cats because it knows to scan local shapes. If you train a ViT on 10 cat photos, it might fail because it doesn't even know that patch 1 sits next to patch 2. It has to learn the rules of geometry first.

cards.title: Bias is a good thing!
cards.body: In human conversation, bias is usually bad. But in machine learning, inductive bias is a superpower! When you have very little training data, a strong inductive bias (like CNN's locality) prevents the model from guessing wild, impossible relationships.

left.text:
Assumes locality & geometry
Requires less training data
Locked into sliding-window processing

right.text:
Assumes nothing about layout
Requires massive training data
Can learn any arbitrary relationship

exercises.prompt: What is 'inductive bias' in machine learning?
exercises.explain: Inductive bias represents the built-in constraints or rules of a model that help it learn patterns more easily.

options.text: The prior assumptions a model makes to generalise from training data to new inputs
A deliberate error introduced into the gradients to slow down training
The preference of a model to always output the majority class
The process of cropping an image during data collection

exercises.prompt: True or false: Vision Transformers have a stronger visual inductive bias than CNNs.
exercises.explain: False. ViTs have very weak visual inductive bias, meaning they do not assume pixels are spatially related by default.
Translation invariance means a CNN detects a feature regardless of its position in the image.
exercises.sentence: CNNs make assumptions about locality and translation ___, making them highly effective on small datasets.

exercises.prompt: Which of these traits makes a CNN easier to train on a tiny dataset of 100 images?
exercises.explain: CNNs assume adjacent pixels are highly related, which limits their search space and makes learning from small data possible.

options.text: Its built-in assumptions about spatial locality restrict the patterns it needs to search for
Its ability to ignore the order of patches completely

exercises.prompt: If ViTs have almost no visual inductive bias, how do they manage to achieve state-of-the-art results on image classification?

options.text: By training on massive datasets (millions of images) so the model can learn spatial geometry rules purely from experience.
By secretly using convolution filters inside the transformer blocks.
By running only on black-and-white input patches.
By hardcoding the position embeddings so they can never be updated.

exercises.explain: Splendid! Given enough data (like JFT-300M), a ViT learns the laws of spatial structure from scratch, allowing it to eventually outperform CNNs. Next: data requirements in depth!
